couple of concerns regarding the proposed six-meter waterline length
threshold.
This limit remains a barrier to achieving a truly
effective safety culture for a couple of reasons.
Firstly, vehicle length is not a proxy for risk.
While smaller craft capsize more easily, fatalities are frequent on
larger vessels. I'm sure you've been provided statistics from the
Safer Boating Forum and the Coastguard
that 93 people drowned on recreational vessels over
66 meters in the last 25
years. And at least 20, 33% of recorded
influence incidents involved vessels eight meters or longer.

In terms of has Genesis contracted any
LNG? No. The answer's no. We're waiting for the procurement process to play
out as everybody else is.
But there hasn't been any discussion about minimum offtake?
Not to my knowledge, no.
Okay. One of the questions I think so that-
We can take one more question, yeah.
One of the questions that everybody has is, and I
don't know if you can answer it, so it's really whether there's been any
discussions with you around it, is whether or not this will be,
whether you have a stake in the investment, who will decide when to
use it, how that's going to play out.
Have there been any discussions with government in how this is going to
factor into the market from you as the
owner of the asset that would be running it? Has that been discussed?
So again, if I go back to our equity raise in March this year,
we were clear with the market that we wouldn't be an investor in an LNG
terminalSo our capital wouldn't be going into an LNG
terminal,
that we would bring Huntly firming options to market if
LNG became available in the market.
But we have no confirmation to that-
Yeah
... so we haven't brought any HFOs forward at this point.
Yeah.
Why aren't you an investor in LNG?
We are ultimately focused on our
portfolio, which is investing in building our renewable
generation, and leveraging our existing assets at
Huntly.
Thank you.
Okay, cool. Scott,
I just want to return to Carlos's question about hardship that
we're seeing with high electricity prices and
high fuel prices,
and your concern about people decarbonizing.
Really good to hear that you're working with Chris with Ecovolt, but we've
heard from Meridian, for example.
Meridian are putting in $5 million into energy hardship programs, $1
million a year distributed through community energy network members.
He's shown an openness to discussing this with other
gentailers to bulk funding that.
Mm-hmm.
So I'm wondering what your openness is to helping
those out of quite a healthy dividend that you're able to
provide,
providing a slice of that, say $5 million,
to match the Meridian contribution to the community energy
network members who are working in our communities.
They help insulate homes, help people in extreme hardship, put
in ventilation
with black mold-
Mm-hmm
... curtains.
Let's keep the question tight, Scott.
Is there an appetite there? I know you've done some work in energy hardship, but
rather than these bespoke things, wouldn't it be nice
if there was a combined approach?
So Genesis has a coordinated program around that.
We invest between $5 and $8 million a year
into those programs. We prioritize the communities
that we have generation sites in. There's a wide range
of that intervention.
That includes curtain banks, energy
support, et cetera,
direct investment into
solar projects at a community level.
We also have our Power Shout program, which is several
hundred thousand hours a year of free power, and our customers
can donate those to households in hardship.
I don't have the figure here with me right now in terms of what last year's
donations were, but we make a significant donation into that
ourselves.
So just as a follow-up. The question really is about, and
I realize that different gentailers have their own programs.
It's not around their own programs because part of the problem
that we hear from the community sector is that there's a heavy
admin cost of using programs
from the different gentailers
that could go to just delivering at the frontline.
And if those
resources were put into a bulk
energy wellbeing fund
working through community energy network members,
it wouldn't necessarily go to Genesis customers, wouldn't necessarily go to
Meridian customers. It would go to people in hardship,
but it would help us reduce
the energy poverty, the extreme distress we see people in-
Mm-hmm
... if there was that willingness to work more as
a gentailer collective. Is that something that you would consider
Look, I think we're open-minded to all options.
We are committed to our existing programs at the moment, like we have
multi-year commitments to not-for-profit electricity
retailers, our own communities, our own community programs, et
cetera. But we're always open-minded in terms of how we do
our bit, whether that is collectively or individually.
We're not sitting here going, "We won't work with the others."
We're just simply saying that at the moment,
that is not a pathway that is under active discussion.
We are focused on the commitments that we have at the moment to
supporting our communities where we operate.
Thanks, Scott.
Can I just very quickly very quickly-
Scott, the problem is you're taking a blooming long time to answer the question.
This is my question.
Okay. Two seconds.
Just to say, look, if you're open to it, can we meet and talk?
Sure.
Thank you. Okay, good. That was quick.
Keep them that way. Okay, Carlos.
Thank you. My question is,
there's quite a lot of
business actually invest in solar farm these days.
But I actually heard from multiple dependent energy generator that
the current market structure and the PPA are favor the
big gentailers, and you guys dominant the market and
also only relied on the power as
only a backup suppliers. What is your respond to this concern?
Can I just ask how
are PPAs favoring the gentailers?
May I say, a lot of feedback coming up is you guys
only buy energy from them when you need them, so you're not
going to provide them a sustainable or long-term contract.
So that create very unfavorable
business environment for those small solar farms because you
always choose when to buy it or when not to buy the energy.
So just so I understand the question, your question is that we only buy the
energy when we need it.
This is what I hear from a lot of small solar farm.
So why would we buy energy when we don't need it?
Well, that's the thing, right? When you sign the contract with them, so
this is why I said it's not favorable to them
because you guys dominant the market.
You guys have all the structure.And you only use
them because on the government point of view, we want more competition.
We want more energies being produced to fit
the market. But the way you guys
sign the agreement with them or the way you
dominate the market, actually, you basically kill off all these
businesses
and make them unavailable.
That's quite a significant allegation.
Every generator in New Zealand is required to sell all of their
electricity to the wholesale electricity market-
Mm
... and to buy back what they need.
Yeah.
So I'm not understanding if an independent generator-
Mm
... wants us to buy energy we won't need so their business works,
how does that work?
So you guys-
I'm not understanding what you're saying.
Yes, but a lot of them, they actually sign an agreement that
you
help you to take on the energies they generate,
but only do it when you really
need it. And-
So are you saying that our shareholders should underwrite their financial
returns, and we should take risk on energy we don't need?
Well, I understand on that aspect, but I'm just saying that is it because of that
you guys already dominant the market, so that create
unfair competition ground for all the
new entrants or all the new businesses set up in this sector.
So Genesis is 15% of the electricity market in New Zealand, so we
don't dominate anything.
But I'm not-
Well, the big four company actually dominate the market
... I'm still not understanding why it would be in our shareholders' interest
to buy energy we don't need.
Can I
put it this way and ask you a question, which I think will
hopefully help with that?
Okay.
So if we have a, an... Because there's one of the questions in there about
intermittent generation.
Mm-hmm.
If we have a vast proliferation of
solar generation-
Mm-hmm
... no batteries, let's say,
and they want to sell to the market.
There is a certain point in time, presumably, which people say, "Well, look, that's
very nice. You've got it operating during the day in the fine weather,
but we don't actually want all of that power then because we've got other things
which are generating then, which also generate at other times as well."
Yep.
That'd be fair.
Yeah, so ultimately, New Zealanders want to buy energy for
breakfast and dinner, and solar generates energy for lunch.
Yeah.
Okay? So there's a value mismatch that's there.
Electricity is a system that's either 100% on or it's
not on at all.
And so when you generate electricity from solar, it has to
go to market or you have to be able to store it somewhere.
That's what batteries are for. But I would just
say that anyone can come to market and offer
anything to anyone. It's a free and open and very transparent
market. And to be honest, it's a very difficult
market to make a return on at a generation asset level.
The returns are 8 to 9%.
Okay, can I ask a couple questions and then I'll go to Laura, and then I'll come to
you, Megan. So just in the written answers
to questions, you've got there the generation stack report, this is
the Transpower one, and the cost per megawatt
of installed capacity.
But then there's also factor you've got to take into account there, well,
solar's the cheapest. I'm not picking on solar, I have a little bit, I suppose.
Solar at 1.9, but it's the generate, the actual what you actually get
out of them,
its ability to generate that. What sort of numbers do you usually
apply to the, for your solar, wind, hydro, geothermal,
because-
In terms of the cost to-
No. Well, let's say solar says, 1.9 million per
megawatt-
Yep
... capacity.
Mm-hmm.
But we know that it doesn't generate most of the time.
That's right.
It generates at about-
20% of the year.
It generates, yeah.
Wind generates at about 40 something.
And hydro and geothermal, if they need to, are up to about 100.
Depending on rain.
Close to, yeah.
It won't be 100, it'll be a bit-
Yeah, that's in the 90 to 100.
But it'll be-
So when you're looking at the cost per megawatt hour generated, it's a very
different number than the
boilerplate number-
Yep
... of theoretical capacity.
Yep. The capital cost is quite different as well.
Oh, yes. No, I see that. But those are the capital cost development per, you've
given us per megawatt capacity as
opposed to actual megawatt hour generated.
Okay, look, two questions if I can at this stage.
One of them is pricing trajectory, because you've talked about, there's a question
there that talks about the,
how do we best reduce... The question was how do we best reduce electricity prices?
And then the answer was, how do we reduce the fastest way to lower
total energy costs? Those are two different things, as you've obviously described
at the beginning.
Yes.
What's the trajectory looking like for electricity costs?
Because I understand it's downwards.
Mm-hmm.
Downwards, and you're nodding. And when do we start to see
electricity companies sending out, or would it be the retail side, obviously,
sending out
notices. Normally, we're used to them saying a notice to say your power price
is going to go up. When do we start seeing some saying, "Well, look, the wholesale
price is starting to trend down. We are going to reduce your
electricity bill
beginning of next month or beginning of the following month." Because I think that
would be a very welcome thing.
Yeah. So-
So when do we start seeing that?
Yeah. So if you look at the components of an electricity bill, the
32% that represents electrons-
Yep
... is certainly stable now and likely to
not have upward cost pressure in it for the foreseeable
future, and that is because of the billions and billions of dollars
that is being invested in new electricity generation.
And if you read the capital market reports, you'll see that the risk
they're alive to at the moment is
overbuild.
Their concern is we're building too much generation at the
moment.The other part of the bill, the
38% that relates to lines charges-
Mm-hmm
... that will continue to go up for this pricing period.
So lines charges are priced and approved by the Commerce Commission every five
years.
Yes, understand that.
Those parts of your bill will continue to go up.
We have no reason to believe GST is going to change.
All right.
And most retailers are driving their operating costs down.
So as we start seeing, because it's usually you guys
are picked on as being the problem in terms of pricing.
As we see further notices go to customers, whether it's
business or retail, presumably there was something saying actually the price of
the electricity component is coming down.
Look, I can't predict what the future is.
Well, but-
On that basis, because we price about six months in advance.
Yeah, well-
But the fundamentals of the wholesale electricity market have
downward pressure in them.
That's exactly what I'm saying, that if you can't predict the future, the wholesale
market is, and you're part of the wholesale market.
That's right.
So you're predicting the future is lower electricity price, the electron bit.
So you can say to the notice, it's coming down, it's the
lines part which is going up, and then people can actually pay attention.
If something fundamentally disrupts the build program that's in play at the
moment, that'll change that dynamic.
Yeah.
If something disrupts the demand side, it will
change that dynamic. It is a very simple commodity of supply
and demand.
Yeah. And look, you also touched my last question here at this stage.
Oh, actually, no, well, I'll ask a different one.
Regional cost variation.
Yep.
To what degree do we see regional cost variation, and what's the cause of
that? Because my understanding is it's because there is a lack of generation
in that particular region.
We've got, like, 10 minutes left.
Okay.
I'm three years into this sector and still don't understand the answer to that
question.
Oh, good. Okay. We'll have to have a further conversation outside.
I'll arrange to come and see you.
Yeah. Had a good conversation with Transpower on that.
Yeah.
So yeah, okay. I think, Laura, did you have a question?
Just really quickly.
And then I'll go back to you, Ben.
My question is, what consenting changes would
allow
new projects and new generation?
It's got the RMA changes going through.
Mm-hmm.
Is there anything that you foresee would be a
decent change that could actually unlock a lot of that potential
opportunity?
I think the Transpower queue is the,
the grids, the transmission queue is the next
big thing to unlock.
Consenting regimes are speeding up, which is
helpful.
But connecting, it's a bit like building a shopping center
and not being able to connect to the main road.
Yep.
And so connecting to the main road is the next big area that would assist.
Would you say, particularly in our lines and that transmission part, that we've
got quite a deficit as a country?
I wouldn't say deficit,
other than you've got a massive step
change in the building program in New Zealand.
And that's put a lot of pressure right through the system.
And so you're in the messy middle of the energy transition in New
Zealand at the moment. And we're going to have bumps
in that messy middle, and not everything moves
in a linear way.
Mm-hmm.
And so as we've all decided to invest billions of
dollars quickly,
that flows on to the need to get electrons onto the superhighway
and then distribute it out to homes and businesses across New Zealand.
Okay. Rika.
Thank you.
Just
kind of round the houses, four
things. One is the Bioenergy Taskforce, that
Genesis was part of, that was set up to
ensure no unnecessary barriers to getting the
supply chain really in place at pace.
What specific policy or regulatory barriers
have been identified through that work, and what responses
as a member of the task force have you received on that?
Sitting here, I don't have detailed knowledge on those outputs.
Be good to have that back.
Happy to follow back with you afterwards.
Come back.
What I would say is economics is the biggest challenge in that at the moment.
Great.
We had some talk earlier in this hearing about
the need
to ship people off gas,
and to get the best value for New Zealand off gas.
Of course, we did have a scheme in place under the previous government.
The government investment in decarbonizing industry, 119
businesses were transitioned away from
gas and coal. Is it your opinion that
having some form of partnership between government and
industry would accelerate the move of
many of our businesses off gas and onto more secure
and more affordable fuels?
Whether we like it or not, as a country, we're getting off gas because we're
running out of it. That's
just a fact at the moment.
The
area that we're seeing in our customer base at the moment,
is the decision between moving to
LPG or moving to electricity.
So if you are a business
or a household that uses low to medium heat
ranges,
LPG is a realistic alternative,
and the conversion is often cheaper to LPG than it is to
electricity. And so that would be the area that we're seeing at the
moment where the tension on moving off gas for a lot of homes and
businesses is sittingSo if you want to move to LPG, you
disconnect your gas and you put in a regulator and some bottles,
and you can run your hot water heating and your heating and
your cooking and all that as normal pretty much.
So that's capital constraints stopping that then?
Yeah, it's the capital constraint.
Whereas if you want to electrify everything, you've got to change your heater,
change your hot water, change your cooking, et cetera.
That is more expensive at the moment than changing your gas
supply.
Thank you. The next thing is, I did say it was around the houses.
So Genesis does have quite a program around battery
electric storage systems, BESS. You've got the 100
megawatt and 200 megawatt at Huntly.
Mm-hmm.
Coming online 2027, and then a stage two.
Two, yeah.
Can you give us a bit of an update on that?
And one of the very detailed questions, which I'm not sure that you can answer,
is whether or not these BESS
systems are going to be set up to do frequency moderation
in the way that we've relied on our hydro system to do it.
But as we expand, whether those systems will be kind of doing
both that storage and frequency moderation.
Yep. So let me start with the back end of that question.
Huntly Power Station is regularly used as a frequency moderator-
... because of its location in the upper North Island next to those big population
centers.
Yeah.
So that is an important job-
Yeah
... that Huntly does. In terms of whether we can use our BESS to do
that, I don't have the answer for you-
Okay
... right here.
In terms of our strategy towards batteries, we are
looking at the ability to store energy across minutes, hours, days, weeks, and
months across our portfolio.
Batteries are minutes and hours. And we are
approaching it in three different levels.
So first of all, the big grid scale batteries that are going
into Huntly, which is 400 megawatt hours of battery
storage. Huntly has a 1400 megawatt grid connection, so
it has an enormous off-ramp and an enormous on-ramp into the
electron superhighway in New Zealand.
The second thing is all of our wind and solar farms are being
consented and designed to have smaller scale batteries
installed down the track. And all of the balance, all of
the transformers that we put in, are built for future battery
installation.
And then at a third level, we are the largest distributed energy retailer in
New Zealand, over 34,000 rooftop solar battery
customers.
5% of our generation was purchased off our customers in January, for
example. And as
that distributed storage starts to become more proximate,
that is going to be a critical part of our short-term energy storage
strategy as well.
So just on that, perfect segue because my next thing was
demand response and DER.
Yep.
So in terms of
what are you doing in terms of
smoothing that peak, what are some of the initiatives?
Gridsmart obviously is something-
Yep
... that you've got underway, but in terms of what we can be doing
more in terms of solar batteries,
and to what extent when your households are going
solar, are they matching batteries with that, and
are those batteries contributing to our demand
response?
Mm-hmm.
Are you kind of marshaling them as a national reserve of
batteries?
And also connected to that is smart charging for
EVs-
Yep
... and what we're seeing there.
Yeah. So,
as I said in my opening remarks, electrifying
transport is the
win here. Rooftop solar with batteries is relevant, but
it's not the big win.
From this year, you will have credible EV to grid options starting to come
into New Zealand, and so an electric vehicle gives you
a double hit. You get fossil fuels out of your
energy bill, but you also potentially get an energy storage
system that can be used to ride
through the low pricing in the middle of the day and the middle of the night, et
cetera.
So I just really want to reemphasize that because EVs are
sort of like becoming the Swiss Army knife of distributed
energy.
In terms of batteries, it's not easy at the moment
in many parts of New Zealand to be able to coordinate
distributed battery systems.
That is because of the separation between the battery
owner and the technology owner.
We're actually finding more success in hot water cylinders at the moment.
Sorry, can you just clarify the battery owner and the technology owner?
If you go to the Solar Zero
structure, which is-
Oh, okay
... the majority of distributed batteries at the moment.
Panasonic are the software operator, and the battery is owned by a
different company. So if you want to coordinate it, you have to do a deal with
Panasonic, for example. That's just one example.
There are many other examples.
But one of the peakiest uses in the household of electricity is
light bulbs,
and so when we did the trial in Christchurch two winters ago, we actually
measured the impact on peak load, and it's materially
measurable. And so for us, it was cheaper
to buy almost
800,000 light bulbs than it was to keep starting and stopping
Rankines at Huntly Power Station.
So we're looking to smooth out the peaks as a result of that.
Batteries into the system will continue to smooth that peak out
. And so there's no one silver bullet in this.
Yeah.
There's a lot of levers that are being pulled at the moment around
managing the volatility that is in the system, both on a
demand and supply side.
Ingrid's mark, can you give us an update on that?
I can't.
Okay.
Because I don't have one. I'm happy to give you one outside of this, but I'd need
to go and ask some people.
Okay.
Yeah.
Absolutely.
Okay, thanks. I'll go to Carlos.
Thank you. On the pure question-
You might get what you ask
... you keep mentioning that you're-
I'll come to you
... putting shareholders on your
priorities.
So what profit margin are you targeting for, for your shareholders?
So in our equity round that we raised money in
March this year, we said that new investment would
generate between 8 and 10% return on investment.
And that's fairly standard for new generation.
So that is at the low end of infrastructure investment returns
in the world.
But that is the range that we target, 8 to 10%.
So since government are the bigger shareholders, 51% of the
shares.
How can you balance out your shareholders' benefit and social
responsibility?
Do you want to answer the shareholder piece or...?
Well, I think what we do is consider the range
of expectations from all our stakeholders,
and generating a profit in a business like
Genesis is all about returning dividends to
shareholders, but also providing enough retained earnings for us
to have the CapEx to be able to continue with our build
program. So we have to continually balance these
expectations from our shareholders to get that mix right, so that we
can continue to grow the company, but also provide the
returns that they expect.
So outside of the government, 49% of Genesis is held by
private shareholders. A good portion of them, about
30% of our total shareholding, are mum and dad investors,
KiwiSaver investors. I don't know anyone that
wants a zero return on their KiwiSaver account.
But if they do, send them our way, because we'd love to have them as an
investor.
And so return on capital is important.
It's not just important for the government, it's important for the 49% of
Genesis that's not owned by the government as well.
Our social responsibility is taken really seriously.
We invest in the communities we operate in.
Many of our staff are intergenerational staff members.
And we are the largest employer in those communities.
We reinvest into those communities.
We also run very substantial programs to look after our customers.
Customers is where your demand comes from.
Falling demand in the business is a terrible thing to have to deal with, so we want
our customers to grow and be prosperous as well.
So we take both of those things incredibly seriously.
Do you think there's a room to further lower the power cost?
I've already asked that question.
Oh, you asked it?
Yeah.
Yeah. I think the trajectory is downwards, but downwards.
I'm still looking for that. I'll ask one quick one because it is exactly on that,
and then I'll go to Scott. And that is just, if you'd done what Carlos is kind of
implying, which is a much lower rate of return, which was
implicit in the capital raise, so let's say it'd been 4%, for example.
How do you think the capital raise would've gone, and what implication would that
have had for future investment in generation?
I think it would've been a struggle to get the capital raise out the door-
Mm
... at that kind of metric.
Mm.
Because investors have lots of options, and they're looking for a return.
They're also looking for faith in the company to deliver what we said we were
going to deliver with that capital raise.
So-
And what implications would that have had for future investment and generation?
Well, we wouldn't have been able to invest-
We wouldn't have had the CapEx.
We wouldn't have had the capital to be able to do the investment to get to our
Gen35 strategy outcomes that we're looking for.
That's your answer. Scott.
Yeah. You've talked about what happens if demand doesn't turn up, but
also in that clearly you've also set out that demand is going to turn
up because we're electrifying. So, and plenty
of build, but
not quite enough to lower the prices of
electricity generation,
and we know that renewables are the lowest cost.
So
again, you've talked about transparency in the market.
Are you really supportive of the level playing field measures, the
mandatory non-discrimination obligations?
Is this something that you really get behind?
Well, Jen, it-
For making sure that that transparency is there, so we
see what prices are available in the market and that the
independent retailers, for example, can bid into that,
can
know what's there.
The market is very transparent.
Compare it to the gas market, which is not transparent.
Electricity in New Zealand is very, very transparent.
Now, clearly the Electricity Authority is working to make it more
transparent. And we don't fear
transparency.
So you're in support.
We don't fear transparency.
You're in support.
As long as the level playing field is genuinely level
and not tilted.
So you're in support of the mandatory non-discrimination obligations.
We're in support of a transparent market
that is fair to everybody.
On that note, I think we'll say we're at time.
Thank you very much. Really appreciate it.
Thank you.
We've hopefully all learnt a lot.
And there may be some follow-up questions to ask.
Sure.
But I'm still keen on this regional variation of prices, so might
have to come and arrange a-
Yeah
... further conversation.
Like I said, three years into it, and still getting to grips with that one.
Yeah. Okay. No, appreciate that. Thank you all very much.
Thank you.
Thanks.
Thank you.
Thanks, Matt
